front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: 'If your interviewer asks "How do you use AI in your work?" a...'
back: If your interviewer asks "How do you use AI in your work?" and you say "I use Copilot for autocomplete," you've already lost the room. This lesson teaches you to answer that question like a senior engineer.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: 'I'm primarily on G4 — agentic editing. I use it for multi-fi...'
back: I'm primarily on G4 — agentic editing. I use it for multi-file refactors and data-shaping. For one-off questions or unfamiliar APIs I'll drop into a G2/G3 chat. I haven't moved fully to G5 autonomous agents yet because I want to review every diff before it lands.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: 'You know what you want. You ask. The AI produces it. You rev...'
back: You know what you want. You ask. The AI produces it. You review and accept.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: 'You don't know what you want. You explore options with the A...'
back: You don't know what you want. You explore options with the AI as a thinking partner.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: 'The AI generated something. It's 80% there. You and the AI i...'
back: The AI generated something. It's 80% there. You and the AI iterate until it's right.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: 'I think of AI-assisted work in three modes: generation when ...'
back: I think of AI-assisted work in three modes: generation when I know exactly what I want, exploration when I'm trying to choose between approaches, and iteration when I'm refining something that already exists. Most of my real work is iteration.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: How to drop this casually in an interview?
back: > "I'm primarily on G4 — agentic editing. I use it for multi-file refactors and data-shaping. For one-off questions or unfamiliar APIs I'll drop into a G2/G3 chat. I haven't moved fully to G5 autonomous agents yet because I want to review every diff before it lands."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: 2.1. Generation workflow?
back: > You know what you want. You ask. The AI produces it. You review and accept.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: 2.2. Exploration workflow?
back: > You don't know what you want. You explore options with the AI as a thinking partner.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: 2.3. Iteration workflow?
back: > The AI generated something. It's 80% there. You and the AI iterate until it's right.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: How to drop this in an interview?
back: > "I think of AI-assisted work in three modes: generation when I know exactly what I want, exploration when I'm trying to choose between approaches, and iteration when I'm refining something that already exists. Most of my real work is iteration."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: 3.1. What "vibe coding" means at the good end?
back: - Iterating quickly with AI assistance. - Trusting your taste about whether the output looks right. - Skipping ceremony when ceremony doesn't earn its keep. - Letting the LLM type so you can think.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: 3.2. What "vibe coding" means at the bad end?
back: - Shipping code you can't read. - "It works" being the only test. - Not understanding the abstractions the AI introduced. - Copying solutions to problems you couldn't articulate.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: 3.3. How interviewers use the phrase?
back: Interviewers asking "Are you a vibe coder?" are usually asking one of three different questions:

front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: 4.1. Pattern-rich, low-novelty work?
back: - Boilerplate (form scaffolds, CRUD endpoints, type definitions). - Conversions (JSON → TS interfaces, CSS → Tailwind, Python → Go). - Test scaffolding (writing the next test that follows the existing pattern). - Documentation (turning code into prose, generating JSDoc/docstrings).

front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: 4.2. Constrained, well-specified algorithms?
back: - Implementing a known algorithm given a clear spec ("debounce", "trie", "merge sort"). - Glue code between well-known APIs (file → S3, webhook → handler). - Refactors with a clear target ("extract this into a custom hook").

front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: 4.3. Search and synthesis?
back: - "What are the three ways people typically solve X?" - "What does this error message usually mean in Postgres?" - "Compare TanStack Query vs SWR for my use case."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: 5.1. Novel architecture under real constraints?
back: The LLM doesn't know your team's deploy story, your billing tier, your historical war stories about the queue that took down prod, your manager's risk tolerance, your timeline. It will produce a design that looks reasonable in isolation and is wrong for your context.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: 5.2. Cross-cutting refactors that span a long history?
back: Imagine: "rename a field across 60 files, but only in the new code paths, leaving the legacy paths alone." The LLM doesn't have the institutional memory of which paths are legacy. It will rewrite the wrong subset, confidently.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: 5.3. Debugging where the bug is in your understanding?
back: If you're prompting the AI to "fix this race condition" but you've misdiagnosed which two operations are racing, the AI will fix the wrong race condition and the bug will return tomorrow.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: 7.1. The "I love AI" pitfall?
back: You sound like a fan, not an engineer. The interviewer will dig deeper to see if you have any judgment.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: 7.2. The "I'm anti-AI" pitfall?
back: You sound like you're hiding the fact that you don't use it. The interviewer will assume you'll be slower than the AI-fluent candidate.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: 7.3. The "It depends" pitfall?
back: You sound evasive. "It depends" is only a great answer when you immediately follow with the things it depends on.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/02-the-skill-stack.md: Layer 1 — Typing?
back: What it is: keystrokes. Pressing letter keys to put characters in a file.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/02-the-skill-stack.md: Layer 2 — Implementation?
back: What it is: turning a clear specification into working code.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/02-the-skill-stack.md: Layer 4 — Judgment?
back: What it is: deciding which design is right for our situation.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/02-the-skill-stack.md: 4.1. Prompt engineering as engineering?
back: Not "prompt hacks." Real engineering: stable system prompts, structured contexts, deterministic output formats, eval suites that score prompts the way unit tests score functions.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/02-the-skill-stack.md: 4.2. Eval-driven development?
back: Writing test cases (sometimes called "evals") that gauge whether an AI-driven feature is doing the right thing. Includes ground-truth datasets, automated scoring, and human-in-the-loop review.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/02-the-skill-stack.md: 4.3. AI code review?
back: The skill of reviewing AI-generated code with a different mental model than human-generated code. AI tends to make different mistakes (overconfident plausible-but-wrong APIs, missing edge cases the spec didn't mention, security mistakes in copy-pasted patterns). Knowing the failure modes is its own discipline.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/02-the-skill-stack.md: 4.4. Cost and latency thinking?
back: LLM calls cost money and add latency. Engineering features that use LLMs at runtime requires understanding token counts, cache strategies, model tiering ("fast model for the easy cases, smart model for the hard ones"), and graceful degradation.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/02-the-skill-stack.md: 4.5. Risk and safety thinking?
back: Knowing where AI must not be in the loop. PII redaction. Prompt-injection defense. Output validation. Regulated-industry constraints.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/03-the-mindset-shift.md: 'Oh, I'm very careful with AI. I read everything. I run all t...'
back: Oh, I'm very careful with AI. I read everything. I run all the tests. I never just accept things.

back: I deeply understand transformer architecture and use that to inform my prompting.

back: Yeah, I... used AI for some of this, sorry.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/03-the-mindset-shift.md: 2.1. The "let me ask the AI" reflex?
back: The crutch user, when asked a question they don't know the answer to, immediately reaches for the AI before thinking. They don't form an initial hypothesis. They don't articulate what they don't know. They just paste-and-pray.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/03-the-mindset-shift.md: 2.2. The "the AI said so" defense?
back: The crutch user, when asked why their code does X, says: "That's how the AI suggested it."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/03-the-mindset-shift.md: 2.3. The diff-flood acceptance?
back: The crutch user accepts giant diffs without reading them. They watch the AI rewrite 14 files, see "tests pass," and move on. The senior user forces small diffs. They reject "rewrite this whole module" suggestions in favor of "extract this one function" steps they can review.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/03-the-mindset-shift.md: 2.4. The novelty assumption?
back: The crutch user assumes anything the AI suggests is current best practice. The senior user knows the AI's training data lags reality by months — sometimes years — and that "best practice" is contextual anyway.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/03-the-mindset-shift.md: 5.1. Form a hypothesis before you prompt?
back: Before typing a single character to the AI, write down (in your head or in a comment) what you think the answer is. Even a wrong hypothesis is useful — it gives you a baseline to compare the AI's answer against.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/03-the-mindset-shift.md: 5.2. Always read the diff?
back: Even tiny diffs. Especially tiny diffs — the small ones are where sneaky behavioral changes hide. Make this a reflex, not a chore. Senior engineers can spot a one-character bug in a 200-line diff because they've read enough diffs.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/03-the-mindset-shift.md: 5.3. Run the code before trusting it?
back: The AI is confident even when wrong. The compiler isn't. The test runner isn't. Run the code. Run the tests. The number of senior engineers who've shipped a confident-looking AI output without running it is not zero, and they'll all tell you the same horror story when you ask.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/03-the-mindset-shift.md: 5.4. Keep a log of AI mistakes?
back: For one week, write down every time the AI was wrong. The format: what you asked, what it returned, what was wrong, what the right answer was. By Friday you'll have a personal failure-mode catalog. It will be your best interview prep artifact.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/03-the-mindset-shift.md: 8.1. The hypothesis log?
back: For the next 5 working days, write down your hypothesis before prompting the AI. At the end of the week, look at the log. How often were you right? How often was the AI? What patterns of disagreement do you see?

front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/03-the-mindset-shift.md: 8.2. The diff drill?
back: Pick the next 10 AI-generated diffs that land in your code. Force yourself to articulate what each one changes, line by line, before accepting it. It will feel slow. After 10 diffs, it will be habit.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/03-the-mindset-shift.md: 8.3. The story file?
back: Open interview-stories.md. Write down 5 STAR-format stories from your real work in the last 6 months. At least 2 of them should feature a moment where you did not trust the AI and made the call yourself.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/03-the-mindset-shift.md: 9.1. The performative caution?
back: > "Oh, I'm very careful with AI. I read everything. I run all the tests. I never just accept things."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/03-the-mindset-shift.md: 9.2. The performative skepticism?
back: > "AI is interesting but I generally write everything myself."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/03-the-mindset-shift.md: 9.3. The performative depth?
back: > "I deeply understand transformer architecture and use that to inform my prompting."

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/README.md: 'Before you can defend AI-assisted development in an intervie...'
back: Before you can defend AI-assisted development in an interview, you have to be able to define it precisely. This chapter gives you that definition — and the vocabulary that goes with it.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: 'My take is that AI has changed what I spend time on, more th...'
back: My take is that AI has changed what I spend time on, more than it changed whether I'm needed. The typing got automated. The judgment didn't. If anything, the judgment got more important because there are more options to choose between.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: 'From a hiring-manager perspective, why should I prioritize a...'
back: From a hiring-manager perspective, why should I prioritize a human engineer over scaling AI tooling for my existing team?" "How do you think about the ROI of a senior engineer vs an AI subscription?" "If we doubled our AI tooling spend, what would still be missing?

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: 'AI tools are force multipliers. Force multipliers don't help...'
back: AI tools are force multipliers. Force multipliers don't help if you don't have force. The engineer is the force — the judgment about what to build, the design choices about how it fits, the accountability when it breaks. AI subscription doesn't show up to the postmortem. I do.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: 'Are you worried about your career?" "Do you think this profe...'
back: Are you worried about your career?" "Do you think this profession will exist in 10 years?" "How do you tell a junior developer to start a career in 2026 with all this happening?

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: 'There are seven things humans still bring that AI does not. ...'
back: There are seven things humans still bring that AI does not. Let me run through them quickly.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: How it sounds?
back: > "Honestly, I look at this stuff and I wonder why we hire any developers anymore. Convince me." > > "Half my team is being asked to do twice the work because of AI. Are you worth what we pay engineers?" > > "I've seen a single founder ship an entire SaaS app with AI alone. So why do I need a team of you?"

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: Tone signals?
back: - The word "convince", "justify", "prove", "worth". - A short, blunt question without the usual interview pleasantries. - Eye contact that's holding you accountable, not warmly engaging. - They lean back, not forward.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: What they actually want?
back: They want to feel that you take the question seriously and have an answer. They don't want a lecture about transformer architecture or a defensive rant. They want a senior engineer's quiet confidence.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: The opening sentence?
back: > "That's a fair question and I think about it a lot. The short answer is that AI raised the floor of what's possible — but it didn't lower the ceiling of what's required from a senior engineer. Let me explain."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: What not to do?
back: - Don't smirk. - Don't deflect ("that's a complicated question..."). - Don't argue with the premise ("AI hasn't actually replaced developers...") — that's a fight.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: How it sounds?
back: > "What's your take on the whole AI-and-developers conversation?" > > "How do you think your job has changed in the last 18 months?" > > "Where do you see the most leverage from AI in your day-to-day, and where doesn't it help?"

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: Tone signals?
back: - "Your take", "how do you see", "what's interesting to you". - Open posture, leaning in. - A genuine pause for you to answer, not a setup for their next point.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: What they actually want?
back: This is the most generous framing. The interviewer is curious, conversational, and probably as uncertain as you are. They want a thoughtful exchange, not a defended thesis.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: The opening sentence?
back: > "My take is that AI has changed what I spend time on, more than it changed whether I'm needed. The typing got automated. The judgment didn't. If anything, the judgment got more important because there are more options to choose between."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: What not to do?
back: - Don't over-prepare and deliver a monologue. They want a chat. - Don't be too humble ("oh, I'm still figuring it out") — you'll sound junior.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: How it sounds?
back: > "Where do current AI tools break down for you, technically?" > > "What's a class of problem you would not let an LLM solve in production?" > > "How do you reason about when to trust LLM output vs verify it manually?"

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: Tone signals?
back: - They use precise vocabulary: "in production", "verify", "class of problem". - Specific examples or follow-up questions are likely. - They're often a senior IC, not a manager.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: What they actually want?
back: They want concrete technical opinions backed by examples. They are the easiest framing to nail because they're testing your engineering thinking, not your soft skills.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: The opening sentence?
back: > "There are three classes of work where I won't let the LLM be the primary author: anything that touches money, anything that requires reasoning across more than ~200 lines of unfamiliar context, and anything where the cost of a wrong answer doesn't show up in tests. Let me give you an example for each."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: What not to do?
back: - Don't be vague. Technical interviewers eat vagueness alive. - Don't say "it depends" without immediately specifying what it depends on.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: How it sounds?
back: > "From a hiring-manager perspective, why should I prioritize a human engineer over scaling AI tooling for my existing team?" > > "How do you think about the ROI of a senior engineer vs an AI subscription?" > > "If we doubled our AI tooling spend, what would still be missing?"

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: Tone signals?
back: - Words from the budget conversation: "ROI", "priority", "spend", "hire". - Often a director, VP, or non-IC. - They're pattern-matching against other hires they've made.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: What they actually want?
back: They want the answer they can paraphrase to their own boss when asked why they hired you instead of buying more AI seats. Give them the line they'll repeat.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: The opening sentence?
back: > "AI tools are force multipliers. Force multipliers don't help if you don't have force. The engineer is the force — the judgment about what to build, the design choices about how it fits, the accountability when it breaks. AI subscription doesn't show up to the postmortem. I do."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: What not to do?
back: - Don't go technical. They don't care about transformer hallucination rates. - Don't get philosophical. They want concrete value. - Don't be modest. This is the framing where confidence is most rewarded.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: How it sounds?
back: > "Are you worried about your career?" > > "Do you think this profession will exist in 10 years?" > > "How do you tell a junior developer to start a career in 2026 with all this happening?"

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: Tone signals?
back: - The question is broader than the role. It's about the field, the future, careers. - Often slightly personal — they're vulnerable too, even if they're hiring. - Pause-laden, less briskly transactional than the hostile framing.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: What they actually want?
back: They want a sober, honest, optimistic answer. They want to feel the energy in the room shift from anxious to constructive. Done well, this is the framing where you most build genuine rapport.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: The opening sentence?
back: > "I'm thoughtful about it but not worried. The work is changing — not vanishing. The people who will struggle are the ones who tied their identity to typing code. The people who'll thrive are the ones who tied their identity to making good engineering decisions. I'm in the second camp, and I'd advise a junior to be in the second camp too."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: What not to do?
back: - Don't catastrophize. - Don't be saccharine ("I think humans will always have a place...") — sounds rehearsed. - Don't pivot too quickly to mechanics. This framing wants 30 seconds of genuine reflection first.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: 'AI is genuinely good at producing text that looks like code....'
back: AI is genuinely good at producing text that looks like code. Producing text isn't the same as engineering. There are seven things engineering requires that AI does not do — and probably won't, in the time horizons that matter for this hiring decision. Let me run through them.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: 'AI optimizes the spec it's given. Deciding what the spec sho...'
back: AI optimizes the spec it's given. Deciding what the spec should be is engineering.

back: AI knows code. Engineers know this codebase, this team, and this customer.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: 'AI suggested I use Redux for the state management on a new m...'
back: AI suggested I use Redux for the state management on a new module. In our codebase Redux had been deprecated for 18 months in favor of Zustand. The AI couldn't know that. A new hire who blindly accepted that suggestion would have wasted a sprint adding a deprecated dependency.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: 'AI can list trade-offs. AI doesn't make trade-offs, because ...'
back: AI can list trade-offs. AI doesn't make trade-offs, because it doesn't own consequences.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: 'AI is great at the next 200 lines. Engineering is great at t...'
back: AI is great at the next 200 lines. Engineering is great at the next 2 years.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: 'AI suggested we store user picks as comma-separated strings ...'
back: AI suggested we store user picks as comma-separated strings to keep the API simple. That works at 8 users. At 80 users we'd have been parsing strings on every read. I made the call to use a normalized schema. AI optimized for the prompt; I optimized for two years from now.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: 'Software is mostly built in conversations. AI is not in thos...'
back: Software is mostly built in conversations. AI is not in those conversations.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: 'PM asked for a feature 'by Friday.' I noticed the real const...'
back: PM asked for a feature 'by Friday.' I noticed the real constraint was a customer demo on Tuesday. The right move was a smaller scope shipped Monday rather than the full feature shipped Friday. AI doesn't catch that — there's no prompt that surfaces a demo two states away.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: 'AI can write code. Engineers know whether the code is good f...'
back: AI can write code. Engineers know whether the code is good for our situation.

back: AI generated a function that walked the data twice — once to filter, once to map. Functionally fine. For 8 teams. We had a planned migration to 800 in Q2. I rewrote it as a single pass, not because the original was 'wrong', but because I could see the next refactor.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: 'At the end of every PR is a human signature. The signature i...'
back: At the end of every PR is a human signature. The signature is the value.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: 'AI raised the floor of what's possible. It hasn't lowered th...'
back: AI raised the floor of what's possible. It hasn't lowered the ceiling of what's required.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: 'It is. So is the bar for senior engineers. Five years ago a ...'
back: It is. So is the bar for senior engineers. Five years ago a senior was someone who could ship features fast. Today they have to do that and judge AI output, and design for AI-assisted teams, and evaluate when AI shouldn't be in the loop. The job got harder, not easier.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: 'Sure, partly. I'd be lying if I said I had no skin in the ga...'
back: Sure, partly. I'd be lying if I said I had no skin in the game. But I'm not asking you to take my word — I'm describing observable, daily failures of AI output that I and every other engineer I know spent today fixing. The case rests on the data, not on my motive.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Soundbite?
back: > "AI optimizes the spec it's given. Deciding what the spec should be is engineering."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Concrete example?
back: > "On my last project, the PM asked for a 'scoring system.' The naive AI prompt would have produced a perfectly working scorer. But the actual job was to choose between three definitions of 'score' — points-per-pick, accuracy-percentage, or playoff-style — each of which served a different storyline for the user. I went and watched the league members react to mockups before any code got written. AI didn't sit in those meetings."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Soundbite?
back: > "AI knows code. Engineers know this codebase, this team, and this customer."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Elaboration (30 seconds)?
back: AI has the public internet's worth of code in its head. What it does not have: your codebase's local conventions, your team's deploy story, the war story from last quarter when the queue overflowed, the senior engineer who refuses to touch the legacy module, the customer who pays you 40% of revenue and hates change. Those facts shape every real engineering decision. AI's suggestions are correct in the average codebase. Your team is not the average codebase. Adapting AI suggestions to local context — knowing what to keep, what to throw out, what to reshape — that's the work.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Concrete example?
back: > "AI suggested I use Redux for the state management on a new module. In our codebase Redux had been deprecated for 18 months in favor of Zustand. The AI couldn't know that. A new hire who blindly accepted that suggestion would have wasted a sprint adding a deprecated dependency."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Soundbite?
back: > "AI can list trade-offs. AI doesn't make trade-offs, because it doesn't own consequences."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Elaboration (30 seconds)?
back: When you make a trade-off — pick A over B — you accept that A's downsides will surface in production, in code review, in the on-call channel at 2am. The AI does not show up to those moments. It doesn't get paged. It doesn't get reviewed. It doesn't have a reputation that suffers when the trade-off ages badly. Engineering decisions live with their consequences for years. The accountability for those consequences is the most valuable thing humans bring. When the postmortem asks "why did we choose this?", the AI cannot answer. You can.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Concrete example?
back: > "We had to choose between caching at the CDN, the application server, or the client. AI laid out the three trade-offs cleanly. The decision required knowing that our CDN bill was already over budget, that our app servers had spare CPU but tight memory, and that 30% of our users had unstable connections. None of that was in the AI's prompt. We picked the application-server caching. When it later created a memory pressure issue, I owned the decision — and the fix."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Soundbite?
back: > "AI is great at the next 200 lines. Engineering is great at the next 2 years."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Elaboration (30 seconds)?
back: LLMs operate on a context window. Your project operates on a multi-year arc. Decisions you make now — schema design, API contract, module boundaries — get harder to reverse with every passing month. The skill of saying "this looks fine for the demo but it'll be technical debt for the next five years, let's spend an extra hour and do it right" is temporal judgment. AI doesn't have it. It doesn't even know the current date with confidence. It cannot weigh present convenience against future maintenance. Senior engineers do that every hour.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Concrete example?
back: > "AI suggested we store user picks as comma-separated strings to keep the API simple. That works at 8 users. At 80 users we'd have been parsing strings on every read. I made the call to use a normalized schema. AI optimized for the prompt; I optimized for two years from now."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Soundbite?
back: > "Software is mostly built in conversations. AI is not in those conversations."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Concrete example?
back: > "PM asked for a feature 'by Friday.' I noticed the real constraint was a customer demo on Tuesday. The right move was a smaller scope shipped Monday rather than the full feature shipped Friday. AI doesn't catch that — there's no prompt that surfaces a demo two states away."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Soundbite?
back: > "AI can write code. Engineers know whether the code is good for our situation."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Elaboration (30 seconds)?
back: AI output is plausibly-correct on the surface and frequently subtly wrong. Spotting the wrong subset requires taste — knowing which patterns wear well, which abstractions leak, which "clean" architectures will fight you in 3 months. Taste isn't innate; it's earned by reading a lot of code, shipping a lot of code, and watching it age. AI does not have taste. It has averages. The skill of "this is correct but it's the wrong correct for us" is irreducibly human.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Concrete example?
back: > "AI generated a function that walked the data twice — once to filter, once to map. Functionally fine. For 8 teams. We had a planned migration to 800 in Q2. I rewrote it as a single pass, not because the original was 'wrong', but because I could see the next refactor."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Soundbite?
back: > "At the end of every PR is a human signature. The signature is the value."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Elaboration (30 seconds)?
back: When code ships, someone owns it. The person on the call. The person who explains it to the auditor. The person whose name is on the postmortem. AI cannot be that person. Not legally, not socially, not professionally. As long as software has consequences — and it always will, especially in regulated, security-critical, or revenue-bearing contexts — there will be a human at the bottom of the trust chain. Engineers are the trust layer. AI tools are below us in the stack, like compilers and linters. Useful, important, not interchangeable with the engineer.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Concrete example?
back: > "I shipped a payments change last quarter. The bug it introduced cost us four hours of partial-charge confusion. The remediation involved a written explanation to compliance, a refund script, and a personal apology to a customer. There is no version of that incident response where 'the AI did it' is an acceptable sentence. I owned it because I had to."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Objection: "But AI is getting better fast."?
back: > "It is. So is the bar for senior engineers. Five years ago a senior was someone who could ship features fast. Today they have to do that and judge AI output, and design for AI-assisted teams, and evaluate when AI shouldn't be in the loop. The job got harder, not easier."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Objection: "Couldn't I just use a junior plus AI for the price of you?"?
back: > "You can. You'll get a lot of typing-rate productivity. The first time the AI's suggestion conflicts with a deploy story, a customer constraint, or a security review, the junior won't have the judgment to push back. That's the sound of technical debt accumulating quietly. I'd argue you save by hiring me; the bill comes due either way."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Objection: "What if I told you AI will fully replace developers in 10 years?"?
back: > "I can't disprove a 10-year prediction. I can tell you that none of the AI-replacement timelines I've seen come from people who are currently shipping production code with AI tools daily. The people closest to the work are the most measured about its limits. I trust that signal more than I trust the timelines."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Objection: "Aren't you just defending your job?"?
back: > "Sure, partly. I'd be lying if I said I had no skin in the game. But I'm not asking you to take my word — I'm describing observable, daily failures of AI output that I and every other engineer I know spent today fixing. The case rests on the data, not on my motive."

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: 'Soundbites win the abstract argument. Stories win the room. ...'
back: Soundbites win the abstract argument. Stories win the room. This lesson gives you eight short, memorable cases — each tied to a pillar from Lesson 2 — that you can adapt as your own.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: 'And of course the AI just made up an API that doesn't exist....'
back: And of course the AI just made up an API that doesn't exist...

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: 'And I, brilliant senior engineer that I am, immediately spot...'
back: And I, brilliant senior engineer that I am, immediately spotted the issue...

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: What happened (template)?
back: You asked the AI to fix a failing test. The AI's diff "fixed" the test by changing the test's assertion to match the buggy code, instead of fixing the bug. The tests passed. The bug shipped.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: 30-second delivery?
back: > "Working late, I asked the AI to fix a failing test. It returned a diff that made the test pass — by changing the expected value to match the broken code's actual output. Tests went green. The bug stayed. If I had accepted that diff without reading it, the bug would have shipped. The AI optimized for what I asked — green tests — not for what I meant — correct behavior. After that I made it a rule: when the AI fixes a test, the diff must touch the implementation, not the assertion."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: Why it lands?
back: It is a tiny, plausible story. The interviewer instantly believes it because they have seen something like it. The lesson is concrete: distinguish passing tests from correct code. It maps cleanly to Pillar 6 (quality judgment).

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: How to make it your own?
back: Find a real instance. Search your git log for phrases like "fix test" or "test now passes". Half of every developer's history has at least one of these moments. Use yours.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: What happened (template)?
back: The AI suggested a popular library to solve a problem. The library had been deprecated months ago, replaced by a newer one. You caught it because you knew your codebase had already moved.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: 30-second delivery?
back: > "AI suggested I use the older state-management library that everyone Googled in 2022. Reasonable in the abstract. Our codebase had migrated off it eighteen months ago — there was an internal RFC and everything. The AI did not know the RFC existed. A new hire would have followed the suggestion and added a deprecated dependency in their first week. I caught it because I had been on the team during the migration. That is the kind of context that does not fit in any prompt I could write."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: Why it lands?
back: It demonstrates Pillar 2 (context) without sounding self-important. The implicit message: I am valuable because I have been here long enough to have caught this.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: How to make it your own?
back: Look for any place in your team's history where a library was swapped, a pattern was deprecated, a custom utility replaced an industry standard, or "we do not do it that way anymore" is true. Every team has these. Mine yours for the story.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: What happened (template)?
back: The AI suggested a quick, denormalized data shape that would have been fine at small scale and painful at larger scale. You picked the slower-now / better-later option.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: 30-second delivery?
back: > "The AI suggested storing user picks as comma-separated strings — one column, simple to read, simple to write. It worked for the eight teams in our beta. We had a planned scale to roughly 800 teams in Q2. At that scale we would have been parsing strings on every read, and adding a 'remove pick X' feature would have been a regex nightmare. I went with a normalized join table. Slower to type, faster to live with. The AI optimized for the demo. I optimized for the next two years."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: Why it lands?
back: Pillar 4 (long-horizon judgment) is one of the harder ones to make concrete. This story does it. The phrase "AI optimized for the demo, I optimized for the next two years" is a quotable line interviewers will remember.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: How to make it your own?
back: Look for any decision where you traded short-term ease for long-term maintainability. Pagination instead of "load all", a normalized DB instead of denormalized JSON, a state library instead of a useState blob. Any of these work.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: What happened (template)?
back: A PM asked for a feature by Friday. You discovered the real constraint was a Tuesday customer demo. You shipped a smaller scope on Monday — useful for the demo — and finished the rest at a sane pace.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: 30-second delivery?
back: > "PM said: 'I need this feature by Friday.' I asked why Friday — turns out there was a customer demo on Tuesday and Friday was just a buffer. The actual ask was: have something demo-able on Monday. We scoped a much smaller version that hit the demo's narrative, shipped Monday, and built the rest at a normal pace through the next sprint. The AI cannot ask 'why Friday?' because it is not in the conversation. The five-minute clarifying question saved a weekend and made the demo tighter."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: Why it lands?
back: This is the most repeatable Pillar 5 story (cross-team translation). It also makes you sound like a senior who manages stakeholders, not just a coder.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: How to make it your own?
back: Look for any case where you reframed a request after a clarifying conversation. The reframing did not have to be dramatic — even "let me make sure I understand the goal" counts. The story lands as long as the clarifying question changed the plan.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: What happened (template)?
back: You shipped a change. It introduced a bug with real customer impact. You owned the response — wrote the postmortem, the customer email, the audit-trail update. The story is not the bug; it is the ownership.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: 30-second delivery?
back: > "I shipped a payments change last quarter. It mishandled a partial-charge edge case and caused four hours of confused customers. The remediation involved a written explanation to compliance, a refund script, and personal apology emails to the affected accounts. There is no version of that incident response where 'the AI did it' is an acceptable sentence. I owned the change because I was the one who hit merge. That ownership — not the typing — is what I am hired for."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: Why it lands?
back: This is the strongest Pillar 7 (accountability) story. It works in any framing because every interviewer respects engineers who own their incidents. Critically, it lets you talk about a bug without sounding like you ship bugs — because the focus is on the response, not the failure.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: How to make it your own?
back: Find an incident from your real history. Tell the version where you owned it well. If you have only ever shipped flawless code, find a friend's incident and tell theirs (with credit) — but ideally find your own. Postmortems are gold for interviews.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: What happened (template)?
back: A caching decision had three valid options. The AI listed them. The decision required knowledge AI did not have. You made the call, owned it, and adjusted when reality bit back.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: 30-second delivery?
back: > "We had to choose where to cache: CDN edge, application server, or browser. AI laid out the three trade-offs cleanly. The decision needed three pieces of context the AI did not have: our CDN bill was already over budget, our app servers had spare CPU but tight memory, and 30 percent of our users had unstable connections. We chose application-server caching. It later created a memory pressure issue, and the fix was a TTL change. I owned the decision and the fix. The AI listed; I chose."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: Why it lands?
back: Pillar 3 (trade-off ownership) is abstract until you tell a story like this. The list of three context items — budget, memory, network reliability — is the punchline. None of those facts come from a prompt. They come from being on the team.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: How to make it your own?
back: Find a decision in your work that had three or more valid technical answers. Trace the decision to facts that lived outside the code: budget, headcount, regulatory constraints, customer mix, deploy story. Those facts are the story.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: What happened (template)?
back: The PM asked for "a scoring system." Three different scoring designs were equally valid. You went and watched users react to mockups before any code was written. The right design surfaced from the research.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: 30-second delivery?
back: > "PM asked for 'a scoring system.' AI could have produced any of three working scorers — points-per-pick, accuracy-percentage, or playoff-style — and each was correct in isolation. They were not interchangeable for the user, though. I built three quick mockups, watched five league members react to each, and discovered the playoff-style version generated the most table conversation. We shipped that one. None of the user reactions were inferable from the prompt. They came from being in a room with humans."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: Why it lands?
back: Pillar 1 (problem framing) is the highest-altitude pillar and the most senior-coded. This story shows you operating there: you went upstream of the spec, did discovery, and let it shape the implementation.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: How to make it your own?
back: Look for any feature where you did not start with "implement X" but with "what should X be?" Even informal user research counts: a Slack poll, a hallway conversation, a five-minute prototype shown to a teammate. Any of those replace pure prompting.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: What happened (template)?
back: You needed to change behavior in a module that had been load-bearing for years. AI tried, but the change rippled through code paths it could not see. You had to step in, scope the change manually, and ship in pieces.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: Why it lands?
back: This story carries Pillars 2 and 4 (context + long-horizon). It also tells the interviewer you can work safely in legacy code, which is one of the most valuable senior signals.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: How to make it your own?
back: Find your equivalent legacy module. Every codebase has one. The story does not need to be dramatic — even "I scoped down what the AI suggested because two of those files have weird history" is enough.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: 12.1. Making the AI sound dumb?
back: > "And of course the AI just made up an API that doesn't exist..."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: 12.2. Making yourself sound heroic?
back: > "And I, brilliant senior engineer that I am, immediately spotted the issue..."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: 12.3. Telling stories you cannot defend?
back: If your story includes "and we used Postgres-specific extension X", be ready for: "Which one? Why?" Do not tell stories whose details you cannot improvise around. Stick to your real history.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: 12.4. Making them too long?
back: A 30-second story stays a story. A 90-second story becomes a monologue. Cut ruthlessly. The interviewer can ask follow-ups for detail. Your job is the headline.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/01-anatomy-of-a-prompt.md: The verb library?
back: | If you want... | Use this verb | | -------------- | ------------- | | Code that does not exist yet | Create / Generate / Add | | Code that exists but should change | Refactor / Replace / Rewrite | | Code split into smaller code | Extract | | Understanding | Explain / Walk through | | Choices laid out | Compare / List approaches to | | A bug found | Diagnose / Trace | | A bug fixed | Patch (with the bug specified) | | Tests added | Cover / Test | | Names changed | Rename |

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/01-anatomy-of-a-prompt.md: Good?
back: > "Add a <LoadingState> component to components/ui/loading-state.tsx. Default export. Props: label (optional, defaults to 'Loading...'), size ('sm' | 'md' | 'lg', defaults to 'md'). Render a centered spinner with the label below it."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/01-anatomy-of-a-prompt.md: What to specify?
back: - Where it lives. File path or directory. - What it is. Component / function / hook / type / config / test. - Its name. Pin the name to avoid the model inventing a verbose one. - Its API surface. Props, parameters, return type. - Its rendering or behavior. One sentence is enough.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/01-anatomy-of-a-prompt.md: Examples that work?
back: - "Do not introduce new dependencies." - "Do not change the existing styling." - "Do not modify any file outside components/ui/." - "Do not introduce a Context — pass props." - "Do not generate tests in this turn — I will ask separately." - "Do not refactor unrelated code." - "Do not change the public API of useSnookerScores." - "Do not add comments unless I ask."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/01-anatomy-of-a-prompt.md: Why this matters in interviews?
back: Interviewers reading your prompts can tell who has been burned by AI sprawl. Constraint clauses are the scar tissue of experienced AI users. Show your scars.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/01-anatomy-of-a-prompt.md: What NOT to include?
back: - Your whole codebase. The model gets confused by noise. - Irrelevant history. "Previously we tried X..." → only mention if the failed attempt is informative. - Personal context. "My PM is annoying about this..." → the model does not need this.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/01-anatomy-of-a-prompt.md: Examples?
back: - "The empty array case must render an explicit empty state — not just nothing." - "Negative numbers should not be possible — clamp to zero in the helper." - "This API call may return 404 if the league has not started — handle that explicitly." - "The user might have unfinished picks. Treat unfinished as a third state, not as wrong." - "Avoid Date.now() — use the clock parameter that is passed in for testability."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/01-anatomy-of-a-prompt.md: Common formats and when to use each?
back: | Format | When | | ------ | ---- | | Unified diff | Refactor / patch / change to existing file | | Single complete file | Brand-new file | | List of file paths + contents | Multi-file scaffold | | Explanation prose, no code | "Compare three approaches" | | Step-by-step plan | Before any code is written, for big features | | Shell commands | Setup, install, scaffold tasks | | JSON block | Structured data extraction |

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/01-anatomy-of-a-prompt.md: Why this matters?
back: Without a format, the model picks one. Half the time it is wrong for your workflow. Specifying the format makes the output immediately usable — paste-friendly, diff-applicable, or grep-able.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/01-anatomy-of-a-prompt.md: v1 — Add the deliverable?
back: > "Add a loading state to <PrimaryButton> in components/ui/PrimaryButton.tsx. When isLoading is true, show a spinner inside the button and disable it."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/01-anatomy-of-a-prompt.md: v2 — Add constraints?
back: > "Add a loading state to <PrimaryButton> in components/ui/PrimaryButton.tsx. When isLoading is true, show a spinner inside the button and disable it. Do not change the existing prop API. Do not add a new dependency for the spinner — use <Spinner> from the same folder."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/01-anatomy-of-a-prompt.md: v3 — Add failure modes?
back: > "Add a loading state to <PrimaryButton> in components/ui/PrimaryButton.tsx. When isLoading is true, show a spinner inside the button and disable it. Do not change the existing prop API. Do not add a new dependency for the spinner — use <Spinner> from the same folder. The button must remain keyboard-focusable while loading (focus ring visible) but click handlers should be no-ops. Announce the state change with aria-busy."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/01-anatomy-of-a-prompt.md: v4 — Add output format?
back: > "Add a loading state to <PrimaryButton> in components/ui/PrimaryButton.tsx. When isLoading is true, show a spinner inside the button and disable it. Do not change the existing prop API. Do not add a new dependency for the spinner — use <Spinner> from the same folder. The button must remain keyboard-focusable while loading (focus ring visible) but click handlers should be no-ops. Announce the state change with aria-busy. Return a unified diff. No prose."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/01-anatomy-of-a-prompt.md: 13.1. Over-specifying?
back: A prompt with twelve constraints, three pages of context, and seven failure modes is overkill. The model gets confused. Stop at six components, with two or three items per component.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/01-anatomy-of-a-prompt.md: 13.3. Mixing modes?
back: > "Refactor this and also add tests and explain how it works and update the docs."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/01-anatomy-of-a-prompt.md: 13.4. Prompting before thinking?
back: The fastest way to write a slow prompt is to skip the thinking step. Spend 30 seconds composing a hypothesis ("I think this should be X") before writing the prompt. The hypothesis improves the prompt.

back: Twelve prompts cover ninety percent of real engineering work with AI. This lesson is the library. Memorize the shapes. Adapt the words.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'I want to build a [DOMAIN] app. The data is [DATA SHAPE IN O...'
back: I want to build a [DOMAIN] app. The data is [DATA SHAPE IN ONE PARAGRAPH]. The user can [3 TO 5 USER ACTIONS]. Do not write code yet. Confirm you understand the data and the actions, propose a tab UI, and call out anything ambiguous.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'My first prompt on a new project is always a no-code prompt....'
back: My first prompt on a new project is always a no-code prompt. I tell the model what I think the problem is and ask it to push back. Forces ambiguities to surface in 30 seconds instead of after I have shipped the wrong thing.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'Define TypeScript interfaces for [ENTITIES]. Strict mode — n...'
back: Define TypeScript interfaces for [ENTITIES]. Strict mode — no any, no implicit optional fields. For each interface, comment any non-obvious invariant. Use composition rather than inheritance when interfaces share fields.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'Strict types up front are the cheapest tax I can pay. They m...'
back: Strict types up front are the cheapest tax I can pay. They make every subsequent prompt easier because the model has shapes to refer to.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'Write [N] pure functions that take [INPUTS] and return [OUTP...'
back: Write [N] pure functions that take [INPUTS] and return [OUTPUTS]. No side effects. No I/O. No framework imports. Add JSDoc comments explaining each. Output as a single .ts file.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'I write the pure logic before any UI. It is the part of the ...'
back: I write the pure logic before any UI. It is the part of the codebase that survives every refactor, and it is trivially testable. I will not start UI work until the logic is solid.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'Render the simplest possible visualization of [DATA]. Use a ...'
back: Render the simplest possible visualization of [DATA]. Use a functional React component with explicit props typed from [TYPE FILE]. No useState. No useMemo. Inline styles. Do not extract sub-components yet.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'I always start with the smallest thing that proves the data ...'
back: I always start with the smallest thing that proves the data is real. Inline styles. No abstractions. Then I let abstractions earn their way in.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'This is the most useful prompt in my library. The 'do not re...'
back: This is the most useful prompt in my library. The 'do not redesign' clause and the 'stop if more than three files' clause are scar tissue from the times the AI helpfully rewrote my whole module.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'Refactor [CURRENT FILE] into an [TARGET PATTERN]. Move only ...'
back: Refactor [CURRENT FILE] into an [TARGET PATTERN]. Move only [WHAT MOVES]. Do not change behavior. Do not change the public API of [PUBLIC SURFACE]. Keep all styling identical. Add no new dependencies.
