back: Refactor components/SnookerFantasyLeague.tsx into an orchestrator pattern. The orchestrator owns activeTab and selectedTeam with useState. Tab components receive props. Do not change behavior. Do not change styling. Tests must continue to pass.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'Refactors are the prompts where I am strictest about constra...'
back: Refactors are the prompts where I am strictest about constraints. Behavior must not change. Styling must not change. Public API must not change. Without those clauses, the AI helpfully 'improves' things and the diff becomes unreviewable.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'Bug: [SPECIFIC OBSERVED BEHAVIOR]. Expected: [SPECIFIC EXPEC...'
back: Bug: [SPECIFIC OBSERVED BEHAVIOR]. Expected: [SPECIFIC EXPECTED BEHAVIOR]. Reproducer: [STEPS]. Hypothesis: [YOUR GUESS]. Confirm or refute the hypothesis. Then patch the smallest part of the code that fixes it. Do not change tests.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'I always include my own hypothesis when debugging with AI. E...'
back: I always include my own hypothesis when debugging with AI. Even a wrong hypothesis is useful — it gives the model something to confirm or refute. Without it, the model guesses, and guessing is what got me into the bug.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'Write [N] [unit / integration / E2E] tests for [TARGET]. Cov...'
back: Write [N] [unit / integration / E2E] tests for [TARGET]. Cover these specific cases: [LIST]. Use [TEST FRAMEWORK]. Mirror the style of [EXISTING TEST FILE if any]. One assertion per test.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'Write three Vitest unit tests for scorePick in lib/scoring.t...'
back: Write three Vitest unit tests for scorePick in lib/scoring.ts. Cases: correct pick returns 3; wrong pick returns 1; unplayed match returns null. Mirror the style of lib/utils.test.ts. One assertion per test. Use describe/it. Do not test calculateTeamScores in this turn.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'Tests are where I am most explicit about cases. The model wi...'
back: Tests are where I am most explicit about cases. The model will happily generate twenty tests of weak quality. I prefer three tests that hit the boundary cases I named.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'I need to decide [DECISION]. Constraints: [LIST]. Constraint...'
back: I need to decide [DECISION]. Constraints: [LIST]. Constraints I am not willing to negotiate: [LIST]. List three approaches with their trade-offs. Do not pick one. Surface any constraints I missed.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'When I am genuinely undecided, I tell the model not to pick....'
back: When I am genuinely undecided, I tell the model not to pick. The 'list three approaches' framing forces it to surface options I would have missed. The choice still belongs to me.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'Generate [DOC TYPE] for [TARGET]. Audience: [WHO]. Tone: [ST...'
back: Generate [DOC TYPE] for [TARGET]. Audience: [WHO]. Tone: [STYLE]. Length: [LIMIT]. Cover [SECTIONS]. Do not invent features. If anything is unclear from the code, list questions instead of guessing.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'The 'do not invent features' clause is critical. AI will hap...'
back: The 'do not invent features' clause is critical. AI will happily document behavior the code does not have. The 'list questions instead' clause turns documentation into a sanity check on the code itself.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'Here is the prompt I wrote: [PROMPT]. Here is the output it ...'
back: Here is the prompt I wrote: [PROMPT]. Here is the output it produced: [OUTPUT]. Here is what I actually wanted: [DESIRED]. Suggest a stronger prompt that closes the gap. Explain which of the six prompt components it strengthens.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'Prompt: 'add a loading state to my button.' Output: a new co...'
back: Prompt: 'add a loading state to my button.' Output: a new component called Loader at the wrong path with new dependencies. Wanted: a small change to PrimaryButton that adds isLoading prop. Suggest a stronger prompt and explain which components it strengthens.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'When a prompt produces bad output, the prompt is the bug, no...'
back: When a prompt produces bad output, the prompt is the bug, not the model. I have a meta-prompt that asks the AI to critique my prompt and suggest a better one. It works almost every time.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'Postmortems are one of the highest-value places to use AI. T...'
back: Postmortems are one of the highest-value places to use AI. The structure is well-known, the tone is hard to nail, and the model is great at both. I always edit the draft, but the draft saves me an hour.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: When you reach for it?
back: You are about to start a new project. Before any code, you want to confirm the model understands the problem.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Template?
back: > "I want to build a [DOMAIN] app. The data is [DATA SHAPE IN ONE PARAGRAPH]. The user can [3 TO 5 USER ACTIONS]. Do not write code yet. Confirm you understand the data and the actions, propose a tab UI, and call out anything ambiguous."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Real example?
back: > "I want to build a fantasy snooker league app. The data is eight teams, each picking match winners across five rounds; matches have winners or are unplayed; scoring is 3 for a correct pick, 1 for wrong, null for unplayed. The user can see standings, drill into one team's picks, see analytics charts, and switch rounds. Do not write code yet. Confirm understanding, propose a five-tab UI, and ask any clarifying questions."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Interview soundbite?
back: > "My first prompt on a new project is always a no-code prompt. I tell the model what I think the problem is and ask it to push back. Forces ambiguities to surface in 30 seconds instead of after I have shipped the wrong thing."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: When you reach for it?
back: You know the entities. You want strict types before any UI exists.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Template?
back: > "Define TypeScript interfaces for [ENTITIES]. Strict mode — no any, no implicit optional fields. For each interface, comment any non-obvious invariant. Use composition rather than inheritance when interfaces share fields."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Real example?
back: > "Define TypeScript interfaces for Match, Team, PlayerInfo, ScoreDetail, TeamScores. Strict — no any. Match.winner is optional only when the match has not been played. Team.r1, r2, qf, sf are arrays where index i corresponds to round-N match index i — comment that invariant explicitly."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Interview soundbite?
back: > "Strict types up front are the cheapest tax I can pay. They make every subsequent prompt easier because the model has shapes to refer to."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: When you reach for it?
back: You want testable, side-effect-free functions before any React.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Template?
back: > "Write [N] pure functions that take [INPUTS] and return [OUTPUTS]. No side effects. No I/O. No framework imports. Add JSDoc comments explaining each. Output as a single .ts file."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Real example?
back: > "Write two pure functions: scorePick(pick: string, match: Match): number | null and calculateTeamScores(team: Team): TeamScores. scorePick returns 3 if pick equals match.winner, 1 if pick is wrong, null if match has no winner. calculateTeamScores aggregates across all five rounds. No I/O. No React. Output as lib/scoring.ts."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Interview soundbite?
back: > "I write the pure logic before any UI. It is the part of the codebase that survives every refactor, and it is trivially testable. I will not start UI work until the logic is solid."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: When you reach for it?
back: You have data and types. You want the smallest possible thing on screen to prove the data is real.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Template?
back: > "Render the simplest possible visualization of [DATA]. Use a functional React component with explicit props typed from [TYPE FILE]. No useState. No useMemo. Inline styles. Do not extract sub-components yet."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Real example?
back: > "Render a basic StandingsTab from an array of TeamWithScores. Use a plain HTML table. No useState, no useMemo, no extracted sub-components. Show team name and total. Inline styles only — match the green/yellow palette in lib/constants.ts."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Interview soundbite?
back: > "I always start with the smallest thing that proves the data is real. Inline styles. No abstractions. Then I let abstractions earn their way in."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: When you reach for it?
back: You have a working component. You want to add one feature without redesigning anything.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Template?
back: > "Add [ONE FEATURE] to [EXISTING COMPONENT at FILEPATH]. Do not redesign anything. Do not refactor unrelated code. If the change touches more than three files, stop and tell me which files first. Return a unified diff."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Real example?
back: > "Add a per-round column to the standings table in components/tabs/StandingsTab.tsx. Show R1 / R2 / QF / SF / Final scores. Do not redesign the table. Do not extract sub-components. If this touches more than three files, stop and tell me. Diff format."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Interview soundbite?
back: > "This is the most useful prompt in my library. The 'do not redesign' clause and the 'stop if more than three files' clause are scar tissue from the times the AI helpfully rewrote my whole module."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Template?
back: > "Refactor [CURRENT FILE] into an [TARGET PATTERN]. Move only [WHAT MOVES]. Do not change behavior. Do not change the public API of [PUBLIC SURFACE]. Keep all styling identical. Add no new dependencies."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Real example?
back: > "Refactor components/SnookerFantasyLeague.tsx into an orchestrator pattern. The orchestrator owns activeTab and selectedTeam with useState. Tab components receive props. Do not change behavior. Do not change styling. Tests must continue to pass."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Interview soundbite?
back: > "Refactors are the prompts where I am strictest about constraints. Behavior must not change. Styling must not change. Public API must not change. Without those clauses, the AI helpfully 'improves' things and the diff becomes unreviewable."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: When you reach for it?
back: A test is failing or a behavior is wrong. You want a hypothesis-driven fix.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Template?
back: > "Bug: [SPECIFIC OBSERVED BEHAVIOR]. Expected: [SPECIFIC EXPECTED BEHAVIOR]. Reproducer: [STEPS]. Hypothesis: [YOUR GUESS]. Confirm or refute the hypothesis. Then patch the smallest part of the code that fixes it. Do not change tests."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Real example?
back: > "Bug: scorePick returns 1 for unplayed matches. Expected: it should return null. Reproducer: scorePick('Williams', { p1: 'Williams', p2: 'Wakelin' }). Hypothesis: the function checks 'pick === winner' before checking 'has winner', and falls through to the 1-return. Confirm or refute. Patch only lib/scoring.ts. Do not modify tests."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Interview soundbite?
back: > "I always include my own hypothesis when debugging with AI. Even a wrong hypothesis is useful — it gives the model something to confirm or refute. Without it, the model guesses, and guessing is what got me into the bug."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Template?
back: > "Write [N] [unit / integration / E2E] tests for [TARGET]. Cover these specific cases: [LIST]. Use [TEST FRAMEWORK]. Mirror the style of [EXISTING TEST FILE if any]. One assertion per test."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Real example?
back: > "Write three Vitest unit tests for scorePick in lib/scoring.ts. Cases: correct pick returns 3; wrong pick returns 1; unplayed match returns null. Mirror the style of lib/utils.test.ts. One assertion per test. Use describe/it. Do not test calculateTeamScores in this turn."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Interview soundbite?
back: > "Tests are where I am most explicit about cases. The model will happily generate twenty tests of weak quality. I prefer three tests that hit the boundary cases I named."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: When you reach for it?
back: A decision needs to be made. You want options and trade-offs without a default pick.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Template?
back: > "I need to decide [DECISION]. Constraints: [LIST]. Constraints I am not willing to negotiate: [LIST]. List three approaches with their trade-offs. Do not pick one. Surface any constraints I missed."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Real example?
back: > "I need to decide where to cache: CDN, application server, or browser. Constraints: total monthly cost cap is $X, app servers have spare CPU and tight memory, 30% of users are on flaky networks. Constraints I am not willing to negotiate: no third-party CDN beyond our existing Cloudfront. List three approaches with trade-offs. Do not pick one. Surface anything I missed."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Interview soundbite?
back: > "When I am genuinely undecided, I tell the model not to pick. The 'list three approaches' framing forces it to surface options I would have missed. The choice still belongs to me."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Template?
back: > "Generate [DOC TYPE] for [TARGET]. Audience: [WHO]. Tone: [STYLE]. Length: [LIMIT]. Cover [SECTIONS]. Do not invent features. If anything is unclear from the code, list questions instead of guessing."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Real example?
back: > "Generate a README for the lib/scoring.ts module. Audience: a junior engineer joining next month. Tone: technical but friendly. Length: under 300 words. Cover the two functions, the invariant about array index correspondence, and one example call each. Do not invent features. List anything unclear instead of guessing."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Interview soundbite?
back: > "The 'do not invent features' clause is critical. AI will happily document behavior the code does not have. The 'list questions instead' clause turns documentation into a sanity check on the code itself."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: When you reach for it?
back: A prompt of yours produced bad output. You want a better prompt.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Template?
back: > "Here is the prompt I wrote: [PROMPT]. Here is the output it produced: [OUTPUT]. Here is what I actually wanted: [DESIRED]. Suggest a stronger prompt that closes the gap. Explain which of the six prompt components it strengthens."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Real example?
back: > "Prompt: 'add a loading state to my button.' Output: a new component called Loader at the wrong path with new dependencies. Wanted: a small change to PrimaryButton that adds isLoading prop. Suggest a stronger prompt and explain which components it strengthens."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Interview soundbite?
back: > "When a prompt produces bad output, the prompt is the bug, not the model. I have a meta-prompt that asks the AI to critique my prompt and suggest a better one. It works almost every time."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Template?
back: > "We shipped a bug: [DESCRIBE]. Customer impact: [DESCRIBE]. Root cause: [WHAT YOU FOUND]. Detection: [HOW WE FOUND IT]. Remediation: [WHAT WE DID]. Generate a postmortem document with Summary, Timeline, Root Cause, Contributing Factors, What Went Well, What Could Improve, Action Items. Be honest about what we missed. No blame language."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Real example?
back: > "We shipped a payments bug: partial-charge edge case mishandled. Impact: 4 hours, ~50 affected accounts. Root cause: missing null check after gateway response. Detection: customer support ticket, then alerting fired. Remediation: hotfix + refund script. Generate a full postmortem doc. Honest about what we missed. No blame."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Interview soundbite?
back: > "Postmortems are one of the highest-value places to use AI. The structure is well-known, the tone is hard to nail, and the model is great at both. I always edit the draft, but the draft saves me an hour."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 16.1. Wrong pattern for the job?
back: Using the Refactor template when you really need a Design template — you got code when you wanted options.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 16.2. Stuffing two patterns into one prompt?
back: "Refactor X and add tests and update the docs" — three patterns crammed into one. Output is mediocre across all three. Run them as three prompts.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 16.3. Skipping the pattern step?
back: Prompting from a blank cursor instead of starting from a template. Slower, weaker output. Even five seconds of "which pattern is this?" improves the prompt.

back: Self-rate 1-5. If <4, re-read the lesson.

back: Self-rate 1-5. If <4, re-read the lesson.

back: Self-rate 1-5. If <4, re-read the lesson.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 'Engineers who can reject AI output cleanly are worth more th...'
back: Engineers who can reject AI output cleanly are worth more than engineers who only know how to accept it. This lesson teaches you both the discipline and how to talk about it.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 'Show me you can tell the difference between AI output you sh...'
back: Show me you can tell the difference between AI output you should accept and AI output you should reject.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 'I asked for a debounced search. The diff implemented a throt...'
back: I asked for a debounced search. The diff implemented a throttled search. They look similar; they are not the same. Reject.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 'The diff used getServerSideProps in a Next.js 14 App Router ...'
back: The diff used getServerSideProps in a Next.js 14 App Router project. The right behavior, the wrong era. Reject.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 'I asked to add a prop. The diff touched eleven files includi...'
back: I asked to add a prop. The diff touched eleven files including a 'while we are here' refactor of the parent. Reject and re-prompt with constraints.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 'The diff wrapped my hook in a context provider 'for future f...'
back: The diff wrapped my hook in a context provider 'for future flexibility.' We do not have a use case for that flexibility. Reject — over-engineering.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 'The diff used a one-line ternary with array spread that I ha...'
back: The diff used a one-line ternary with array spread that I had to stare at for two minutes. Reject — even if the spread is correct, code I cannot read in three seconds is code I will misread under pressure.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 'Add a loading state... but also do not change the styling......'
back: Add a loading state... but also do not change the styling... but also use the existing Spinner... but also...

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 'Show me a recent diff from your AI tool that you rejected. W...'
back: Show me a recent diff from your AI tool that you rejected. Walk me through why.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 'How do you handle AI hallucinations?...'
back: How do you handle AI hallucinations?

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 'AI sometimes gives wrong code. Doesn't that worry you?...'
back: AI sometimes gives wrong code. Doesn't that worry you?

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 2.1. The diff does the wrong thing?
back: The most basic reason. The output works, but it is not what you asked for. The model interpreted your prompt differently than you intended.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 2.2. The diff does the right thing wrongly?
back: The output is functionally correct but uses an approach inappropriate for your codebase: deprecated APIs, the wrong abstraction, fighting your existing patterns.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 2.3. The diff is too big?
back: The output sprawls into files you did not ask about. Even if every line is correct, the change is unreviewable. Force a smaller diff.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 2.4. The diff hides complexity?
back: The output works but introduces an abstraction (a wrapper, a context, a generic) you did not ask for and that adds more cognitive cost than it saves.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 2.5. You cannot explain a part of it?
back: The output works and looks reasonable, but there is at least one block you cannot walk through line by line. If you cannot explain it, you cannot ship it.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 3.1. You keep adding "but also..." in follow-ups?
back: > "Add a loading state... but also do not change the styling... but also use the existing Spinner... but also..."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 3.2. The output keeps drifting in the same wrong direction?
back: If three iterations all produce variants of the wrong thing, your prompt is anchoring the model toward that wrong thing. Often a single misleading word is the culprit. "Make this more efficient" often produces premature optimization. "Make this cleaner" often produces unnecessary abstractions. Audit your verbs.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 3.3. You find yourself debugging the prompt instead of the code?
back: If you have spent more than two minutes deciding whether to reject a diff, the prompt was probably under-specified. Reject the diff, rewrite the prompt, run again. Faster than wrestling with the output.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 3.4. The output is plausible but addresses a different problem?
back: The most insidious failure. The diff compiles, runs, and looks good — but it solves a problem adjacent to yours. This means your prompt was ambiguous about the problem itself. Go back and write the problem statement more precisely.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 4.2. Reject in writing, briefly?
back: When you reject a diff, write one line about why. To yourself. To the PR description if relevant. "Rejected because it changed the public API of useScores; constraint added to next prompt." This builds your personal failure-mode catalog and signals the discipline to anyone reading your history.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 4.3. Reject without rage?
back: Frustration is contagious. If you find yourself reflexively dismissing AI output, your prompts will get worse and your suspicion will get less calibrated. Maintain a working assumption that the model is trying to be helpful and that bad output is fixable. That neutrality is what produces clean prompts.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 11.1. Rejecting performatively in interviews?
back: Saying "I reject AI code all the time" without specifics. The interviewer asks for an example, you do not have one, and you sound rehearsed. Always have a real example.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 11.2. Rejecting reactively, not deliberately?
back: Rejecting because the diff "feels off" without articulating why. That feeling is data, but it has to translate to a sentence. Otherwise you cannot prompt better next time.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 11.3. Confusing rejection with failure?
back: A rejected diff is not a failure. It is the review step working as intended. If your tool produces a wrong diff and you catch it, the system worked. The failure case is the wrong diff that ships.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 11.4. Rejecting because of taste alone?
back: Sometimes you reject because the diff is "ugly." That is allowed, but be honest about it. Style preferences are not architectural concerns. Distinguish them when explaining your rejection.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/README.md: 'The interviewer can tell within five lines of a prompt wheth...'
back: The interviewer can tell within five lines of a prompt whether you respect the craft of prompting or treat it as a magic spell. This chapter teaches you to demonstrate the craft.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md: 1.3. AI-Augmented Take-Home?
back: > "Build this small project. AI is fine. Submit a PR within 48 hours plus a 5-minute Loom walking through your decisions."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md: Step 1 (5–10 seconds): Re-state the problem?
back: > "OK, so you want a function that takes [input] and returns [output], and the constraints are [constraint 1] and [constraint 2]. Did I understand correctly?"

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md: Step 3 (20–40 seconds): State your approach in plain English?
back: > "My plan is: define the type first, then write a pure function that handles the happy path, then add the edge cases I just asked about, then write three unit tests covering those cases."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md: Step 4 (40–60 seconds): Open the AI tool, write the first prompt deliberately?
back: Do not paste. Type. Out loud, narrate the components from Lesson 3.1:

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md: 3.1. The pre-prompt narration?
back: Before each prompt, narrate what you are about to ask and why.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md: 4.1. After the prompt produces output?
back: Read it line by line. Out loud. Two of three candidates skim and accept; you read and verify. The interviewer's notes will reflect that.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md: 4.2. Before accepting a refactor?
back: If the AI is changing existing code, always slow down. "Let me read the diff before I accept." The interviewer hears: this person catches accidental changes. Senior signal.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md: 4.3. Right before you say "I think we are done"?
back: Take 30 seconds. Re-read the requirement. Walk through one input mentally. Catch one off-by-one or null-handling miss before the interviewer does. Often the difference between an offer and a "not quite this round."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md: 4.4. When the interviewer says "Hmm..."?
back: If the interviewer makes a hesitant noise, stop typing. Look up. Say: "You sound like you might be seeing something. Want to share?" This converts a potential pothole into a teaching moment for them and survives many close calls.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md: 5.2. The "skip the constraint" speed-prompt?
back: You skip the constraints because you want to move fast. The output sprawls. You spend 10 minutes cleaning up. Net negative.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md: 5.3. The fix?
back: Before each prompt under observation, count to three in your head. "Three. Two. One." Three seconds of breathing space between thinking and typing. It feels long. It is fast.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md: What NOT to do?
back: - Apologize for the AI. "Sorry, the AI is being weird." Sounds like deflection. - Argue with the AI. "No, I told you not to..." — pointless on screen. - Panic-accept. The diff is wrong but you accept it because you do not know what to do. - Switch to typing it yourself silently. The narration died; the interviewer is now staring at your hands.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md: What to do?
back: 1. Pause. "OK, this is not what I asked for." 2. Diagnose out loud. "It overshot — refactored the parent. I will reject this and re-prompt with explicit scope." 3. Re-prompt. Narrate the new prompt's added constraint. 4. Move on.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md: Minute 0:00–1:00 — Re-state and clarify?
back: > "So I need a function: input is a list of users, output is the top 3 by signup recency, filtering out unverified. Two questions: how is signup recency stored — timestamp, ISO string, Date? And if there are fewer than 3 verified users, do I return what I have or throw?"

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md: Minute 17:00–25:00 — Debug?
back: The failing test: returns 3 users newest-first. Debug. The output is correct in count but the order is oldest-first. Look at the function. The sort is wrong direction.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md: 12.1. Over-narration?
back: Talking nonstop. The interviewer cannot think. Pause every 30–60 seconds. Silence is allowed.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md: 12.2. Under-narration?
back: Typing silently. The interviewer cannot read your process. Default to narrating each prompt and each diff review.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md: 12.3. Selling the AI instead of yourself?
back: Saying things like "watch how fast this is with AI". The interview is about you, not the tool. Talk about your decisions; the AI is in the background.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md: 12.4. Hiding the AI?
back: Some candidates open the AI panel offscreen and quietly use it. The interviewer notices. Be visible. The AI is part of your toolset; do not be secretive.

back: What logging would you add to make sure this does not regress silently?

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: 3.1. The plausible-but-fabricated API?
back: The code calls a method that does not exist. Or it calls a method with the wrong parameter order. Or it imports a function from a module that exports something almost-but-not-quite the same name.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: 3.2. The off-by-one in array logic?
back: AI is uneven on array boundary conditions. It will write for (let i = 0; i <= arr.length; i++) or arr.slice(0, n - 1) when you wanted slice(0, n). Often the test for the happy path passes, and the boundary fails.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: 3.3. The "fixed" test?
back: The test was failing. The AI made it pass — by changing the assertion, not the implementation. Tests are now green; behavior is still wrong.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: 3.4. The "almost-correct" regex / SQL / glob?
back: AI is excellent at producing regexes and SQL that look right and silently mismatch. Common: a regex that allows or rejects one extra character; an SQL JOIN that should be LEFT JOIN; a glob that matches *.tsx when the codebase has .ts and .tsx mixed.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: 3.5. The deprecated pattern?
back: The code uses an API that was correct in 2022 and incorrect now. Common in fast-moving frameworks: Next.js pages/ patterns in an App Router project, React componentWillMount, deprecated Node APIs.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: 3.6. The silently-swallowed error?
back: AI loves try { ... } catch (e) {} for any operation that could throw. The error gets swallowed. The bug surfaces three layers downstream as a confused null.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: 3.7. The unintended state mutation?
back: AI sometimes mutates objects passed in as arguments instead of returning new ones. Real-world bug: a Redux reducer that mutates state, breaking React's diff detection.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: 4.1. The first sentence — declare what you are doing?
back: > "OK, before I run anything, let me read what the code says it does."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: 4.3. The confirmation — run a tiny experiment?
back: > "Quickest way to test that — call the function with [specific input], observe [specific output]."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: Step 1 — Read?
back: > "OK, the function takes picks and winners arrays, iterates with i = 0; i <= picks.length, and adds 3 for matches, 1 otherwise. Returns total."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: Step 2 — Hypothesis (plant the flag)?
back: > "My first hypothesis: the loop condition is <= instead of <. That's an off-by-one. It runs one extra iteration where both picks[i] and winners[i] are undefined, undefined === undefined is true, so it adds 3. Test expects 7, got 8. The 8 = 2 correct + 1 wrong + 1 phantom 'correct' from the off-by-one. Hypothesis matches."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: Step 3 — Confirm?
back: > "Let me confirm — running the test mentally: i=0, A===A, +3. i=1, B===X, +1. i=2, C===C, +3. i=3, undefined===undefined, +3. Total: 10. But the test says 8. So my hypothesis is wrong about the magnitude."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: Step 4 — Re-read more carefully?
back: > "OK — the loop condition runs i = 0, 1, 2, 3. At i=3, picks[3] and winners[3] are both undefined. The condition picks[i] === winners[i] is undefined === undefined === true, so we add 3. That should give us 10, not 8. So either I'm misreading or the test framework cuts something off..."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: Step 5 — Fix and narrate?
back: > "Two options for the fix. [A] Change <= to <. [B] Add a length-equality precondition with a thrown error. A is the minimum fix. B is more defensive but assumes the caller might pass mismatched arrays. The function's contract isn't documented, so I'll do A and add a comment that the caller is responsible for matched lengths."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: Re-prompt when?
back: - The bug requires a structural change you do not want to make manually. - The diff would be larger than what is reasonable to type by hand. - The original prompt was missing a constraint and adding it would fix the issue cleanly. - You are short on time and the AI's pattern-matching beats your typing.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: Fix yourself when?
back: - The bug is a single character / single line. - The bug is in code the AI keeps re-introducing despite your prompts. - You need to ensure no surrounding code changes (high blast radius). - You can explain the fix faster than you can write a precise prompt for it.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: The interview soundbite?
back: > "My rule: re-prompt for structural changes I would otherwise have to think hard about. Fix manually for one-character bugs and for high-blast-radius code where I need precision. The rule is not 'always one or the other'; it is 'use the right tool for the change size and blast radius.'"

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: When AI-debugging works?
back: - You give the AI new information it did not have. ("Here is the failing test message: ...") - You give it a tight scope. ("Patch only the loop condition in lib/scoring.ts.") - You explicitly tell it to revert its previous attempt and start over.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: When AI-debugging fails?
back: - You vaguely ask "fix it" — the AI guesses, often the same way as before. - You forgot to mention what's wrong. The AI sees green tests and thinks you are confused. - You let the AI keep iterating without verifying each iteration.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: 10.1. The seven-signature drill?
back: Take a working file from your codebase. Have a friend introduce one of the seven failure modes from section 3 (without telling you which). Time yourself debugging. Repeat with each of the seven.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: 10.2. The fake-AI-output drill?
back: Find a small open-source repo. Pretend an LLM wrote it. Read it as if you were debugging — annotate any place that smells like one of the seven signatures. Compare your annotations to the project's git log; were the warts there real, or did you imagine them?

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: 10.3. The recorded-debug drill?
back: Same setup as Lesson 4.1. Record yourself debugging. Watch back. Note every silent stretch over 15 seconds. Each silent stretch is a missed narration opportunity.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: 11.1. Skipping the "read first"?
back: Under stress, candidates jump straight to running. You miss bugs that the code itself reveals. Read first, run second.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: 11.2. Over-trusting your hypothesis?
back: Two minutes spent defending a wrong hypothesis is two minutes wasted. The 30-second confirmation rule prevents this — if you cannot confirm in 30 seconds, the hypothesis is probably wrong.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: 11.3. Fixing without testing the fix?
back: You found a bug. You patched it. You celebrate. Always re-run. Sometimes the "fix" introduces a different bug. Until the test is green, you are not done.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: 11.4. Ignoring the seven signatures?
back: If you spot a bug pattern in section 3, say its name. The interviewer hears: this person has seen this pattern before. Senior signal.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 2.1. Does it do what was asked??
back: The most basic question. Read the PR description. Read the diff. Are they about the same thing? AI loves to do "more than asked"; juniors accept the bonus work.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 2.2. Does every API call point to a real, current API??
back: Hover over imports. Check that methods exist. AI fabricates plausibly-named methods. A 30-second sanity check on imports catches this most of the time.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 2.3. Does it respect the codebase's existing patterns??
back: Find a sibling file with similar responsibilities. Compare. AI imports its training data's style; your codebase has its own. Mismatch = future maintenance pain.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 2.4. Are there any one-character bugs hiding in plain sight??
back: Off-by-one, wrong comparison operator, missing await, accidentally-async function. AI produces these at a slightly higher rate than careful humans.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 2.5. Were tests added that test the new behavior, not just pass??
back: Open the test file. Read each test. Does it actually exercise the change? Common AI failure: tests that import the new code and assert on a trivial property, giving false confidence.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 2.6. Are there empty try/catch blocks??
back: AI loves swallowing errors. Search for catch in the diff. Every empty body is a flag.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 2.7. Are there any types??
back: In a TypeScript codebase, any is a smell. AI sometimes reaches for any instead of working out the correct type. Each one needs justification.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 2.8. Are there hardcoded values that should be parameters or constants??
back: Magic numbers, hardcoded strings, baked-in URLs. AI defaults to plausible values rather than asking. Each one is a refactor invitation.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 2.9. Was anything deleted that should not have been??
back: Read the diff with an eye on the minus signs. AI is capable of helpfully removing lines it deems unnecessary that turn out to be load-bearing.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 2.10. Does the code match the PR description??
back: If the description says "fix bug X" and the diff also adds an unrelated feature, that is two changes in one PR. Push back.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 2.11. Could you ship this if you owned it??
back: The final gut-check. Imagine the PR is yours. Imagine being on call when it breaks. Would you ship it as-is? If no, articulate why.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 3.1. The "this won't compose" comment?
back: > "This is fine in isolation, but useUserScores is going to be called inside <TeamRow>, which itself is rendered in a list of 50. That means 50 hook invocations per render. We should hoist this up."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 3.2. The "this hides a bug we'll see in production" comment?
back: > "The empty catch on line 47 will swallow the network error. We will not see the failure in our metrics. Either rethrow or log."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 3.3. The "future maintainer will struggle" comment?
back: > "This name useThing will not survive a year. In six months someone will grep for the actual feature and miss this entirely. Suggest useTeamScoreSubscription or similar."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 5.1. The opening?
back: > "OK, let me start with the high-level — read the PR description, then walk the diff. I'll call out things as I see them."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: Structural pass?
back: > "Two files in the diff: the implementation and one test. Description matches scope. No new dependencies. Good baseline."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: Summary?
back: > "To summarize: I'd block this PR on three things — the type, the comparator (which has two bugs), and the missing real tests. I'd note the empty catch and the hardcoded 3 as non-blocking but should-fix. Three blockers, two suggestions."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 7.1. Critique the code, not the coder?
back: The PR allegedly came from "a junior engineer." Do not say things like "this is sloppy" or "the AI clearly made a mess." Talk about the code in third-person, neutrally.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 7.2. Compliment what works?
back: When something is correct, say so. "The scope is appropriately small — that's good." It signals that you read for both wins and losses, not just losses.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 7.3. Pace your delivery?
back: A reviewer who barrels through eight comments in two minutes sounds harsh. A reviewer who takes a breath after each major comment, makes eye contact, and lets the panel acknowledge before moving on — that reviewer sounds senior.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 11.1. The salted PR drill?
back: Take a real, working PR from your repo (open-source or your own). Have a friend salt it with one of the AI failure-mode signatures from Lesson 4.2. Open it cold. Time yourself reviewing. Did you find the salt? How long did it take?

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 11.2. The peer review?
back: Pair with another developer. Each of you submits a small AI-assisted PR to the other for review. Treat it as a real interview format — narrate aloud, written comments after. Switch roles. Watch how each of you reviews differently.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 11.3. The recorded review?
back: Open any open-source PR you do not have context for. Record yourself reviewing it for 10 minutes. Watch back. Note the gaps in your narration and the comments you made that lacked justification.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 12.1. Reading silently?
back: If the panel cannot hear you think, they will not credit you with thinking. Default to narration. Even "Reading the diff..." is enough to fill the air.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 12.2. Diving into the deepest detail first?
back: Some candidates immediately zoom into a regex or a comparator and start picking it apart. Resist. The structural pass first — "is the scope right? Are the right files touched?" — sets you up to be the reviewer the panel wants to hire.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 12.3. Performing skepticism?
back: Some candidates over-criticize because they think it makes them look senior. The opposite. The most senior reviewer says "this part is fine" the most often — they have calibrated the bar precisely.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 12.4. Forgetting to summarize?
back: You spent 10 minutes finding issues. The panel needs a 30-second wrap. Always summarize at the end. Count the blockers. Count the suggestions. Hand it back.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/README.md: 'The interview formats are changing. "Solve this on a whitebo...'
back: The interview formats are changing. "Solve this on a whiteboard, no internet" is dying. "Build this with AI, share your screen" is the new normal. This chapter teaches you to dominate that format.

back: Self-rate 1-5. If <4, re-read the lesson.

back: Self-rate 1-5. If <4, re-read the lesson.

back: Self-rate 1-5. If <4, re-read the lesson.

back: Self-rate 1-5. If <4, re-read the lesson.

front: Can you deliver the section-12 senior soundbite without notes?
back: Self-rate 1-5. If <4, re-read the lesson.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: 'Six categories of decision define a senior engineer's day. A...'
back: Six categories of decision define a senior engineer's day. AI participates in all of them. AI does not own any of them. This lesson is the map.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: 'With AI doing so much, how do you spend your time? What do y...'
back: With AI doing so much, how do you spend your time? What do you actually decide?

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: 'Define a Postgres schema for users, orders, and order line i...'
back: Define a Postgres schema for users, orders, and order line items.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: 'Split this file into four components....'
back: Split this file into four components.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: 'Add error handling to this function....'
back: Add error handling to this function.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: 'AI is a fantastic trade-off-listing machine. It is, by defin...'
back: AI is a fantastic trade-off-listing machine. It is, by definition, not a trade-off-making machine. The trade-off only becomes a trade-off when someone owns the consequences. The AI doesn't carry a pager. I do.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: 'How would this design scale to 1M users?...'
back: How would this design scale to 1M users?

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: 'Generate a migration script that adds this column....'
back: Generate a migration script that adds this column.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: 'You can use an LLM during this interview. When would you?...'
back: You can use an LLM during this interview. When would you?

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: 'If I asked GPT-5 to design Twitter for me, wouldn't I get a ...'
back: If I asked GPT-5 to design Twitter for me, wouldn't I get a reasonable answer?

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: 2.1. Domain modeling?
back: The shape of the data, the entities, the relationships, and the invariants. The AI can propose a schema. Choosing the schema — knowing what to denormalize, what to make immutable, what to version — is yours.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: 2.2. Boundary placement?
back: Where do components, services, and modules end and begin? The AI can split a file at a sensible spot. Drawing the system's seams — knowing which transformation belongs at the API, which at the service, which at the DB — is yours.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: 2.3. Failure-mode design?
back: What happens when this fails? The AI can add a try/catch. Designing for graceful degradation — knowing what should retry, what should fail closed, what should fail open, what should circuit-break — is yours.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: 2.4. Trade-off ownership?
back: When two designs are equally valid, which ships? The AI can list trade-offs. Picking the trade-off — accepting that the choice will be questioned in 6 months when reality bites — is yours.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: 2.5. Scale and cost?
back: Will this hold at 100x the current load? At 0.1x? Sizing the system, choosing the cost-vs-latency curve, knowing when premature optimization actually pays — is yours.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: 2.6. Migration and rollout?
back: How do you ship a change without breaking the world? The AI can produce a migration script. Designing the rollout — feature flags, dual-writes, dark launches, canary fleets — is yours.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: What the AI does?
back: > "Define a Postgres schema for users, orders, and order line items."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: Senior soundbite?
back: > "The AI gives me schemas that work for the demo. The schemas that work for five years require knowing which fields are point-in-time snapshots, which are current values, and which are derived. Those are domain decisions, not database decisions, and they have to come out of conversations with the people who own the business rules."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: Where the AI helps?
back: - Generating the SQL once you've made the decisions. - Listing trade-offs of normalize-vs-denormalize. - Spotting missing indexes once you describe the access patterns.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: Senior soundbite?
back: > "Boundaries are the most expensive thing in any architecture — once a service boundary exists, it is almost impossible to undo. The AI moves files around for me. Choosing where the boundaries are, knowing which seams must never be crossed and which can flex — those decisions live with us for years and are probably 30 percent of what 'architecting' actually means."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: Where the AI helps?
back: - Generating the boilerplate when a boundary is decided (interface, adapter, contract). - Spotting violations of an existing boundary in a PR. - Drafting a deprecation note when a boundary changes.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: Senior soundbite?
back: > "Most architectures' failure modes are designed by accident — whatever the original code happened to do. Designing failure modes deliberately means knowing which calls must succeed, which can drop, which can retry. The AI helps me write the retry loop once I've decided this should be a retry loop. The decision is upstream of the code."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: Where the AI helps?
back: - Implementing retry logic once you've specified the retry policy. - Drafting alert rules from a runbook. - Listing the failure modes in a checklist that you then categorize.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: What only you do?
back: - Pick one. Accountably. - Document why you picked it, in a way that survives the next on-call rotation when someone wonders why we're not doing the obvious other thing. - Carry the explanation through stakeholder pushback ("can't we just...?") with confidence. - Revisit when conditions change. The AI does not know that the cost of option A doubled this quarter.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: Senior soundbite?
back: > "AI is a fantastic trade-off-listing machine. It is, by definition, not a trade-off-making machine. The trade-off only becomes a trade-off when someone owns the consequences. The AI doesn't carry a pager. I do."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: Where the AI helps?
back: - Surfacing trade-offs you might miss. - Estimating the cost / latency / complexity of each option. - Drafting the ADR (architecture decision record) once you've chosen.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: What only you do?
back: - Distinguish expected scale (1M users) from bursty scale (1M concurrent in 5 minutes during a marketing event) from degenerate scale (one bad actor). Each one demands a different design. - Decide which scaling strategies are worth it given your current size. Premature scale work is one of the most common forms of waste. - Trade off cost against latency at each layer. The AI doesn't know your CFO's tolerance. - Decide what to shed when overloaded. The AI does not own the customer's emotional response to a 503.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: Senior soundbite?
back: > "The right scale design is rarely the maximum scale design. Most systems do not need horizontal sharding; most systems would benefit from a cache layer they don't have; most systems will fall over in ways the load test missed. Choosing the right level of scale investment for a given moment in a company's life — that's a judgment about people and budget, not just throughput. AI doesn't see those."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: Where the AI helps?
back: - Sizing calculations once you've stated the load. - Comparing AWS vs GCP vs self-hosted costs at a stated load. - Sketching the failure modes of each scaling strategy.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: What only you do?
back: - Decide whether to dual-write to old and new for a period. - Decide whether to backfill the new column synchronously, asynchronously, or never. - Decide whether to feature-flag the new path. The AI doesn't know your flag system. - Decide whether to canary to 1%, 10%, 100%. The AI doesn't know your incident history. - Decide what rollback looks like. The AI's "if it fails just rollback" is naive — some changes are unrollable once any traffic has used them.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: Senior soundbite?
back: > "Migrations are where engineering meets reality. Every elegant migration on paper has a rollback story that gets told once and never written down. Designing the rollback before the rollout is the discipline. AI helps me with the script. The decision tree above the script is mine — and it's where most production incidents come from."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: Where the AI helps?
back: - Generating the migration SQL or code. - Drafting the dual-write logic once you've chosen it. - Reviewing your runbook for missing steps.

back: Self-rate 1-5. If <4, re-read the lesson.

back: A 60-minute system-design interview, narrated end-to-end, with AI integrated where it helps and excluded where it doesn't. Use this as a reference for any "design X" interview prompt.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md: 'Design a fantasy sports application for one million users. L...'
back: Design a fantasy sports application for one million users. Live scoring, leaderboards, news feed, social features. You have 60 minutes. AI tools are permitted. Walk me through your thinking.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md: 'Quick sanity check — for a fantasy sports app like the one I...'

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md: 'Now the non-functional side. These shape the architecture mo...'

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md: 'Tell me more about the leaderboard....'
back: Tell me more about the leaderboard.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md: 'Quick check — Redis ZRANGE on a sorted set of 1M members, wh...'

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md: 'With time left, let me name the parts of this design I'd mos...'

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md: 'Before I start — am I allowed to use an LLM as a sounding bo...'
back: Before I start — am I allowed to use an LLM as a sounding board during this hour? I'd use it sparingly — for a sanity-check on a number, or to surface trade-offs — but not for the architectural decisions themselves.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md: What you say?
back: > "Before I draw anything, can I confirm the scope? When you say 'one million users,' is that registered total, monthly actives, or daily peak? And by 'fantasy sports' — are we talking pick'em prediction games, or full-roster draft fantasy with trades and waivers? And one more: is there real money on the platform, or is this a free social product?"

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md: The interviewer's likely answer?
back: > "DAU 200k, peak 500k. Pick'em-style, like the snooker app you may have seen. No real money — it's a community product."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md: Where the AI does NOT help?
back: You don't ask the AI clarifying questions. You ask the interviewer. The clarification phase is human-to-human. Reaching for the AI here would signal you don't know who to talk to.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md: What you say?
back: > "OK, given that scope, let me list the functional requirements. I'll bullet them and you can tell me if I miss anything."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md: Where the AI helps?
back: You do not ask the AI here either, but — if you are 50 minutes in and forgot something, you could quickly prompt:

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md: What you say?
back: > "Now the non-functional side. These shape the architecture more than the features do."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md: Where the AI helps?
back: You could sanity-check your latency assumptions with the LLM:

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md: What you say?
back: > "OK, the boxes-and-arrows. I'll start with the data flow for the most performance-critical path — live scoring — and work outward."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md: What only humans do in this phase?
back: - Choose the stream technology. The AI can list Kafka vs SQS vs Redis Streams trade-offs. You pick — based on your team's familiarity, your AWS spend, your latency budget. - Choose the cache topology. Redis cluster? In-process? Edge cache? Each one has consequences for failure modes and dev experience. - Decide which writes go to the stream vs which are synchronous. Pick submission must be synchronous (user expects confirmation). Score updates can be async.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md: Why this is interview-grade?
back: - You named two strategies. - You picked one with a reason rooted in expected scale. - You stated the conditions under which you'd switch. - You noted that the migration is local — meaning your boundary placement was good.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md: Where the AI helps in deep dives?
back: This is the phase where you might genuinely reach for the LLM:

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md: What you say?
back: > "With time left, let me name the parts of this design I'd most worry about and how I'd mitigate each."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md: Why this phase is the most senior phase?
back: Most candidates run out of steam after the deep dive and just answer follow-up questions. You volunteer the risks. That signals: I think about my own designs critically.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md: What you say?
back: > "OK, summary in 30 seconds: the architecture is event-driven scoring, Redis-cached read paths, two boundaries — match service and scoring worker. The known weak points are stream backlog under burst, cold cache on Redis restart, and GDPR deletion of historic-leaderboard data. Things I haven't covered that I'd want to: notifications fan-out, social features, and the admin tooling itself. Want to dig into any of those, or any part of what I sketched?"

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md: 12.1. Over-reliance on the AI?
back: A candidate I watched once asked the AI eleven times in a 50-minute interview. Each ask was a small question. Cumulatively, the panel concluded the candidate couldn't make decisions without the AI. Limit yourself to two or three deliberate uses.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md: 12.2. Drawing without narrating?
back: Silent drawing is the worst possible mode. Even if your design is perfect, the panel can't see your reasoning. Talk.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md: 12.3. Skipping the clarification?
back: Jumping into design before clarification produces a design for the wrong problem. Five minutes of clarification saves twenty minutes of redesign.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md: 12.4. Forgetting to name the pillars?
back: Every system-design hour is a referendum on Lesson 5.1's six categories. If you finish without having explicitly named at least three of them, the panel may not have heard you cover them. Name them as you cover them. "This is a boundary-placement decision..." makes it visible.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 'Every senior interview eventually becomes a trade-off conver...'
back: Every senior interview eventually becomes a trade-off conversation. Not "what would you build?" — but "why this and not that?" This lesson teaches you the language, structure, and pitfalls of those conversations.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: Step 1 — Restate the choice?
back: > "OK — the question is whether to use A or B for [purpose]. Let me make sure I understand both."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: Step 2 — Name the axes that matter?
back: > "There are roughly four axes I'd evaluate this on: [axis 1], [axis 2], [axis 3], [axis 4]."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: Step 3 — Score each option on each axis?
back: > "On [axis 1], A wins because [reason]. On [axis 2], B wins because [reason]. On [axis 3], roughly equal. On [axis 4], it depends on [unknown]."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: Step 4 — Identify the deciding axis for this situation?
back: > "Given the scenario you described — [specific constraint] — I'd weight [axis] highest, because [reason]. That tips the decision toward A."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: Step 5 — State the decision and the reversibility?
back: > "So I'd start with A. If [observable signal], I'd revisit and consider migrating to B. The migration would cost roughly [X], so the cost of being wrong is bounded."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 3.1. Latency?
back: How fast does the user-facing response come back? p50, p95, p99.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 3.4. Complexity?
back: How hard is this for the team to build, debug, and operate? Junior-friendly vs senior-only.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 3.5. Reliability?
back: What's the failure surface? How often does it fall over? How fast does it recover?

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 3.6. Consistency?
back: Strong, eventual, causal, monotonic. What's the user-visible behavior under concurrent updates?

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 4.2. Microservices vs monolith?
back: > "The honest answer is that almost everyone over-builds microservices. Monolith wins on time-to-ship, debugging, and team-cohesion-cost. Microservices win on independent deployment, language flexibility, and bounded blast radius — but only if you have the operational chops for them. > > I'd default to monolith for a team under 30 engineers. Above that, I'd start carving out the parts where independent deploy or different runtime make a real difference — usually the parts at the edge of the system, like ingress or fan-out workers. That's pragmatic microservices, not the full lift."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 4.3. Server-side rendering vs client-side rendering?
back: > "Three considerations: time-to-first-content, SEO, and runtime cost. SSR wins on TTFC and SEO. CSR wins on per-request server cost — once the JS is loaded, the client does the work. > > For a fantasy app, the leaderboard pages benefit from SSR — fast first paint, SEO for shareable URLs. The interactive parts — pick submission, real-time scoring — are CSR-driven. Modern frameworks (Next, Remix, SvelteKit) let me mix the two per route. I'd lean SSR-by-default, hydrate-when-needed."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 4.4. Build vs buy?
back: > "The trap with 'build vs buy' is that engineers default to 'build because we'll customize it perfectly,' and the customization never ends up mattering. The honest axis: how core is this to our differentiation? > > Auth: buy. Stripe / Clerk / Auth0 — never going to be our differentiator, the threat model is hard, every team that's tried has regretted it. > > Scoring engine: build. It's the heart of the product. We'll iterate on it for years. > > Email delivery: buy. SendGrid / Postmark. > > General rule: buy commodity infrastructure, build domain logic."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 5.2. "Premature [X] is the root of all evil."?
back: Use when: someone proposes a complexity that won't pay off for a while. Substitute optimization, abstraction, microservicing, generalization.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 5.3. "Engineering is choosing what to be wrong about."?
back: Use when: a perfectionist on the panel keeps pushing for the most-correct option without weighing costs.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 5.4. "Every architecture has a customer."?
back: Use when: discussing trade-offs that pit developer convenience against user experience.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 5.5. "I'd write the ADR before I write the code."?
back: Use when: discussing how to document a decision. ADR = architecture decision record.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 5.6. "What's the cost of being wrong here?"?
back: Use when: someone is over-thinking a low-stakes decision. Forces them to articulate why this one matters.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 5.7. "If we had to deprecate this in two years, what would that look like?"?
back: Use when: discussing extensibility / boundary placement. The answer reveals coupling.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 5.8. "The hard part isn't choosing — it's communicating the choice to the team."?
back: Use when: the panel asks how you'd roll out a controversial decision. A favorite of staff interviewers.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 6.3. The "AI told me" trap?
back: > "Well, when I asked the LLM, it said B is preferred for this kind of thing."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 6.4. The buzzword trap?
back: > "We'd use a CQRS pattern with event sourcing and saga orchestration."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 7.2. The right response — engaging?
back: > "Interesting — I'd want to understand the case for the other option. What weight are you putting on which axis? My reasoning was [restate], but I might be missing something."

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 12.1. Naming axes you can't actually score on?
back: If you say "latency is one axis" and then can't tell the interviewer what the latencies would be, you've over-reached. Only name axes you can score.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 12.2. Forgetting reversibility?
back: Every architectural decision has a reversibility cost. Name it. "If we're wrong, here's what migration looks like." Senior signal.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 12.3. Treating the trade-off as binary?
back: Often the answer is neither, or both with a clear seam. "We use SQL for the transactional core and Redis for the hot read paths" is a more sophisticated answer than "SQL" alone.

front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 12.4. Not bringing the AI into the conversation?
back: If you're in an AI-permitted interview and you never mentioned AI's role in trade-offs, the panel may wonder if you actually use it. One mention is enough. "In a real working context I'd ask the LLM to surface trade-offs I might have missed — but the decision still has to be mine."

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/README.md: 'System design interviews are the most important hour of the ...'
back: System design interviews are the most important hour of the loop, and AI changes them less than you think. This chapter teaches you to argue with the AI in the room, not for or against it.
